periods.name: Tonight
periods.detailed: Mostly cloudy, with a low around 66. South wind 5 to 14 mph.

periods.name: Tuesday
periods.detailed: A chance of rain showers after 11am. Mostly cloudy, with a high near 78. South wind 14 to 17 mph, with gusts as high as 31 mph. Chance of precipitation is 50%. New rainfall amounts less than a tenth of an inch possible.

periods.name: Tuesday Night
periods.detailed: Showers and thunderstorms likely. Mostly cloudy, with a low around 66. South wind 10 to 16 mph. Chance of precipitation is 70%. New rainfall amounts between a tenth and quarter of an inch possible.

periods.name: Wednesday
periods.detailed: Rain showers likely between 7am and noon, then showers and thunderstorms likely between noon and 4pm, then a slight chance of showers and thunderstorms between 4pm and 5pm. Partly sunny, with a high near 81. Southwest wind around 10 mph, with gusts as high as 25 mph. Chance of precipitation is 60%. New rainfall amounts less than a tenth of an inch possible.

periods.name: Wednesday Night
periods.detailed: Partly cloudy, with a low around 57. West wind 7 to 10 mph. New rainfall amounts less than a tenth of an inch possible.

periods.name: Thursday
periods.detailed: Mostly sunny, with a high near 78. West wind around 8 mph.

key_messages: Scattered showers and thunderstorms Tuesday afternoon and evening, followed by a lower chance on Wednesday primarily in northern and eastern Vermont. Strong wind gusts are possible with a few storms, especially in southern St. Lawrence County.
Blue sky conditions for Thursday with cool and crisp air.
A warming trend at the end of the week followed by a chance of thunderstorms Sunday.
afd.what_changed: As of 302 PM EDT Monday... Thunderstorm risk for Wednesday in Vermont has continued to decrease.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 201902
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
302 PM EDT Mon Jul 20 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
As of 302 PM EDT Monday...

Thunderstorm risk for Wednesday in Vermont has continued to
decrease.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 302 PM EDT Monday...

1. Scattered showers and thunderstorms Tuesday afternoon and 
evening, followed by a lower chance on Wednesday primarily in 
northern and eastern Vermont. Strong wind gusts are possible with a 
few storms, especially in southern St. Lawrence County.

2. Blue sky conditions for Thursday with cool and crisp air.

3. A warming trend at the end of the week followed by a chance 
of thunderstorms Sunday. 

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 302 PM EDT Monday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: Tranquil weather today will become dynamic on Tuesday as 
an unseasonably strong low pressure system (roughly 992 millibars 
over northeastern Ontario) drags multiple fronts through the area 
through Wednesday night. Strong southerly flow between this low 
pressure area and high pressure to our east will transport 
increasing humidity following a couple of dry days, with dew points 
climbing back into the 60s by afternoon. Generally with large scale 
divergent flow ahead of a potent upper level trough, scattered 
showers will develop Tuesday, while thunder risk is limited 
by instability struggling to reach 500 J/kg. Greater 
instability is favored in northern New York where a severe 
thunderstorm risk remains.

Damaging wind gusts are driving the severe weather outlook for 
tomorrow. The risk timing appears to begin roughly 2 to 3 PM, peak 
around 4 to 6 PM, and diminish by 8 PM. Thunderstorms are 
strongly favored downwind of Lake Ontario, then trend southward 
into central New York and points south and east with time. 
Therefore, in our forecast area the chances of these stronger 
thunderstorms is much higher in St. Lawrence County and perhaps 
Franklin County. These areas may have just enough favorable 
ingredients for a localized wet microburst to occur; CAPE should
reach around 1000 J/kg, some mid-level dry air will be present,
and steep 0 to 3 kilometer lapse rates are favored given the 
time of day and deep mixing promoted by the background wind. 
Cell motions will tend to be from southwest to northeast given 
the cloud layer flow, and there could be multiple rounds of 
thunderstorms in this area. The latest HREF local probability 
matched mean shows 6 hour precipitation approaching 3 inches, 
and convection allowing models depict hourly rainfall rates 
between 1 and 2 inches. Altogether, an isolated instance of 
urban/flash flooding (affecting part of a town or two) is 
possible along with the severe wind threat. 

Farther east, peak instability is expected to diminish greatly,
in part due to some stable marine influence from south- 
southeasterly flow and also due to potential daytime cloudiness 
from scattered showers ongoing during the morning to our south. 
As such, risk of thunderstorms of any variety is fairly low 
from the Adirondacks eastward, especially towards northeastern 
Vermont. Even in the most unstable air in our western areas, it 
appears CAPE will be insufficient for supercells. So the 
possibility of severe storms making it farther east is low, 
contingent on cells organizing in a quasi-linear fashion and 
sweeping eastward. That being said, a few rumbles of thunder 
will be possible amongst widespread showers Tuesday evening 
driven by cooling temperatures aloft, but the overlap of 
sufficient moisture and instability is limited and would not 
expect much activity overnight besides widespread showers. 
Rainfall amounts will tend to average from 0.3" to 0.7", with 
the typical locally higher amounts within thunderstorms 
anticipated.

Based on the latest forecast data, the Wednesday convective threat 
for Vermont has trended downward. Most guidance shows isolated
coverage of showers and maybe a thunderstorm or two developing 
ahead of the lagging surface cold front during the midday or 
late afternoon. If, and it's a big if, there is enough 
instability to overcome copious dry air aloft and associated 
poor lapse rates in the mid-levels, ample deep layer ahead of 
the front will support organized thunderstorms that would be 
capable of strong winds, with probabilities increasing in 
eastern Vermont. We'll have to watch trends, but for now it 
looks like a mainly quiet weather day with only slight chances 
for thunderstorms.

KEY MESSAGE 2: An unseasonably cool air mass, with minimal high 
altitude smoke, should make for outstanding weather for outdoor 
activities on Thursday as an upper level trough pushes to our 
northeast. Westerly flow in the morning will probably support some 
rough conditions on Lake Champlain that should improve during the 
day as anomalous low level temperatures moderate along with 
decreasing wind speeds. Expect high temperatures not too far off of 
seasonable norms due to abundant sunshine, currently projected to 
range through the 70s.

KEY MESSAGE 3: A surface high pressure will dominate Friday and 
Saturday, bringing a warming trend with it. Daytime temperatures in 
valley locations will slowly rise from the upper 70s on Thursday to 
the upper 80s by Sunday. We are tracking a low pressure system that 
gets pushed north of our CWA by the upper level ridge. If the 
current forecast holds this system will not impact our CWA. The most 
impactful system in the extended will be an upper level trough and 
associated surface front that comes through on Sunday. This system 
is forecasted to bring a chance of rain showers and a slight chance 
of thunderstorms Sunday into Monday.

&&

.AVIATION /18Z MONDAY THROUGH SATURDAY/...
Through 18Z Tuesday...VFR dominates throughout the day, with just
some fair weather Cu. Otherwise southerly winds with moderate 
gusts throughout the day. Winds will die down overnight, with no
fog development anticipated. Tomorrow afternoon winds will pick
up along with a chance of showers and thunderstorms.

Outlook...

Tuesday Night: Mainly VFR, with local MVFR possible. Definite
SHRA, Chance TSRA.
Wednesday: Mainly VFR, with local MVFR possible. Chance SHRA,
Slight chance TSRA.
Wednesday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday: VFR. Slight chance SHRA.

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...Kutikoff
DISCUSSION...Langbauer/Kutikoff
AVIATION...Langbauer
